questions.question:
A coastal region with unparalleled beauty is home to many species of animals. It is dotted with coral reefs and unspoilt white sandy beaches. It has remained inaccessible to tourists due to poor connectivity and lack of accommodation. A company has spotted the opportunity and is planning to develop a luxury resort with helicopter service to the nearest major city airport. Environmentalists are upset that this would lead to the region becoming crowded and polluted like any other major beach resorts.

Which one of the following statements can be logically inferred from the information given in the above paragraph?

A. The culture and tradition of the local people will be influenced by the tourists.
B. The region will become crowded and polluted due to tourism.
C. The coral reefs are on the decline and could soon vanish.
D. Helicopter connectivity would lead to an increase in tourists coming to the region.
questions.answer: D. Helicopter connectivity would lead to an increase in tourists coming to the region.
questions.to_find: statement that can be logically inferred from the passage

understand.plain: The passage describes a pristine coastal region that is currently inaccessible. A company plans to build a luxury resort with helicopter access. Environmentalists fear that this infrastructure will attract tourists and cause the region to become crowded and polluted. We need to identify what can be directly and logically inferred.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that directly follows from the stated premises without adding new assumptions.
keywords.example: If the passage says better access leads to more tourists, we can infer more tourists will come with helicopter service.

keywords.explain: The region cannot be easily reached because it lacks transport connectivity and accommodation.
keywords.example: Poor road/air connectivity means few tourists currently visit.

keywords.explain: Their worry is that development will cause overcrowding and pollution, but this is their fear, not a stated fact.
keywords.example: Environmentalists predict crowding — that prediction is an opinion, not a logical certainty from facts.

understand.visual_alt: A pristine coastal region with coral reefs, currently inaccessible, about to receive helicopter tourism access.

given.aim: identify the one statement that directly follows from the passage without additional assumptions

terms.meaning: planned transport link from the resort to the nearest city airport
terms.example: This would reduce travel time dramatically and open the region to visitors.
terms.connects: directly improves connectivity, making the region accessible to tourists

terms.meaning: the region cannot be reached easily due to poor connectivity and no accommodation
terms.example: Without roads or lodging, only very few adventurous travellers visit.
terms.connects: adding helicopter access removes the main barrier to tourist visits

terms.meaning: fear that the region will become crowded and polluted like other beach resorts
terms.example: This is a conditional worry, not a stated outcome, so it cannot be directly inferred.
terms.connects: distinguishes between fear/prediction (option B) and logical fact (option D)

given.plan: Eliminate options that introduce assumptions not in the text (A: culture of locals — not mentioned; C: coral reefs declining — not stated; B: region WILL become crowded — stated only as a fear). Option D follows directly: helicopter service improves connectivity, and the passage implies this will increase tourist arrival.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A
steps.apply: Option A states local culture will be influenced by tourists. The passage mentions no local people or cultural impact — this is an external assumption not supported by the text.

steps.title: Evaluate Option B
steps.apply: Option B says the region WILL become crowded and polluted. The passage states only that environmentalists are UPSET and FEAR this outcome. A fear is not a logical certainty.
steps.note: Eliminate B — it restates a concern as a fact.

steps.title: Evaluate Option C
steps.apply: Option C claims coral reefs are on the decline. The passage says the region IS DOTTED with coral reefs — no decline is mentioned anywhere.

steps.title: Evaluate Option D
steps.apply: Option D states helicopter connectivity will increase tourists. The passage says: (1) the region is inaccessible due to poor connectivity, and (2) a company plans helicopter service. Logically: removing the connectivity barrier increases tourist arrivals. This is a direct and necessary inference.

solution.result: D. Helicopter connectivity would lead to an increase in tourists coming to the region.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal reasoning question; no mathematical formulas are required.

questions.question:
The Cricket Board has long recognized John's potential as a leader of the team. However, his on-field temper has always been a matter of concern for them since his junior days. While this aggression has filled stadia with die-hard fans, it has always stopped John from converting his raw aggression into big scores. Over the past three seasons though, that picture of John has been replaced by a cerebral, calculative and successful batsman-captain. After many years, it appears that the team has finally found a complete captain.

Which of the following statements can be logically inferred from the above paragraph?

i. Even as a junior cricketer, John was considered a good captain.
ii. Finding a complete captain is a challenge.
iii. Fans and the Cricket Board have differing views on what they want in a captain.
iv. Over the past three seasons, John has rectified his bowling.

A. (i), (ii) and (iii) only
B. (i) and (iii) only
C. (ii) and (iii) only
D. (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv)
questions.to_find: statements that can be logically inferred from the passage

understand.plain: The passage describes John's evolution from a hot-headed junior cricketer with leadership potential to a mature, successful batsman-captain. The Cricket Board was always concerned about his temper, while fans loved his aggression. Over three seasons he transformed, and the team now has a complete captain. We must carefully distinguish what is directly inferable from what is assumed.

keywords.explain: A statement that must be true given the passage — not something that might be true or is merely implied by suggestion.
keywords.example: 'The team lacked a complete captain for many years' can be inferred from 'After many years, it appears the team has finally found a complete captain.'

keywords.explain: A composed, strategic approach that replaced raw aggression — John matured as a leader and batter.
keywords.example: His change in approach is what made him a complete captain over the last three seasons.

keywords.explain: The board saw the temper as a problem; fans loved the aggression — these are opposite reactions to the same trait.
keywords.example: Board: concern about temper. Fans: filled stadia because of aggression.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline of John's cricketing evolution from aggressive junior to cerebral captain over three seasons.

given.aim: determine which numbered statements follow logically from the passage

terms.meaning: Even as a junior, John was considered a good captain.
terms.example: The passage says the Board recognized his POTENTIAL as a leader, not that he was already a good captain as a junior.
terms.connects: potential is not the same as being considered good — statement (i) is not supported

terms.meaning: Finding a complete captain is a challenge.
terms.example: The passage says 'After many years, it appears the team has finally found a complete captain' — the phrase 'after many years' implies it took a long time and was difficult.
terms.connects: directly supported by the passage — statement (ii) is valid

terms.meaning: Fans and the Cricket Board have differing views on what they want in a captain.
terms.example: The Board was concerned about his temper; fans filled stadia because of his aggression — they clearly valued different qualities.
terms.connects: directly supported by contrasting reactions described in the passage — statement (iii) is valid

terms.meaning: Over the past three seasons, John has rectified his bowling.
terms.example: The passage says he became a successful BATSMAN-captain; bowling is not mentioned at all.

given.plan: Check each statement against the passage. (i) overstates — 'potential' is not 'good captain'. (ii) valid — 'after many years finally found' signals difficulty. (iii) valid — board concerned about temper, fans loved aggression. (iv) invalid — bowling not mentioned. Only (ii) and (iii) hold.

steps.title: Check statement (i): junior captain
steps.apply: The passage says the Board 'recognized his potential as a leader' since junior days. Recognizing potential is not the same as calling him a good captain. Statement (i) overstates what is written.

steps.title: Check statement (ii): finding a complete captain is a challenge
steps.apply: The passage ends with 'After many years, it appears that the team has finally found a complete captain.' The phrase 'after many years... finally' strongly implies that such a find was difficult and took a long time — a challenge.

steps.title: Check statement (iii): differing views of fans and Board
steps.apply: The Board viewed John's aggression as a 'matter of concern' that stopped him from converting into big scores. Fans loved the same aggression so much that it 'filled stadia.' These are directly opposite valuations of the same trait.

steps.title: Check statement (iv): bowling rectified
steps.apply: The passage describes John as a 'batsman-captain.' There is no mention of bowling anywhere in the passage. This statement introduces information absent from the text.

solution.result: C. (ii) and (iii) only — these two statements are directly supported by the passage.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal reasoning / passage comprehension question; no mathematical formulas are needed.

questions.question:
The new cotton technology, Bollgard II, with herbicide-tolerant traits has developed into a thriving business in India. However, the commercial use of this technology is not legal in India. Notwithstanding that, reports indicate that the herbicide tolerant Bt cotton had been purchased by farmers at an average price of Rs 200 more than the control price of ordinary cotton, and had been planted in 15% of the cotton growing area in the 2017 Kharif season.

Which one of the following statements can be inferred from the given passage?

A. Farmers want to access the new technology if India benefits from it.
B. Farmers want to access the new technology even if it is not legal.
C. Farmers want to access the new technology for experimental purposes.
D. Farmers want to access the new technology by paying high price.
questions.answer: B. Farmers want to access the new technology even if it is not legal.
questions.to_find: statement that can be inferred from the passage about farmers' behaviour

understand.plain: The passage establishes that Bollgard II (a herbicide-tolerant Bt cotton) is commercially illegal in India, yet farmers are buying it at a premium price and planting it on a substantial fraction of cotton area. The key inference is about what this behaviour reveals regarding farmers' attitudes toward the technology's legal status.

keywords.explain: A genetically modified cotton technology with herbicide-tolerant traits that improves weed control and yield.
keywords.example: Farmers prefer it because it reduces labour cost on weeding despite its higher price.

keywords.explain: Despite the fact that; used here to signal that farmers acted even though the technology is illegal.
keywords.example: 'Notwithstanding that it is illegal, farmers purchased it' — their behaviour ignored the legal status.

keywords.explain: An inference follows directly from stated evidence; an assumption adds external information not in the text.
keywords.example: Farmers paying more for an illegal product -> they want it despite illegality is an inference, not an assumption.

understand.visual_alt: Farmers purchasing illegal Bollgard II cotton at premium prices and planting it across 15% of cotton area in 2017.

given.aim: identify which statement logically follows from the farmer behaviour described in the passage

terms.meaning: The technology is illegal for sale/planting in India as a commercial product.
terms.example: Despite this legal prohibition, farmers bought it at Rs 200 premium.
terms.connects: The illegality + purchase decision directly supports option B

terms.meaning: Farmers paid more than the regulated price for ordinary cotton to obtain this technology.
terms.example: They voluntarily paid extra, showing demand and willingness to access the technology.
terms.connects: Willingness to pay more shows desire to access it, but the key word is 'even if not legal'

terms.meaning: Significant adoption — not a small isolated experiment — confirms widespread farmer preference.
terms.example: 15% of total cotton growing area shows this is not experimental use but active adoption.

given.plan: The passage explicitly states two facts: the technology IS illegal, AND farmers ARE buying and planting it anyway. Option B is a direct synthesis of these two facts. Options A, C, and D either add unjustified motivations (benefit to India, experimental purpose) or describe only a partial truth (paying high price is necessary but not the core inference).

steps.title: Identify the key evidence
steps.apply: Two facts stand out: (1) commercial use of Bollgard II is NOT legal in India; (2) farmers purchased it at a premium and planted it in 15% of cotton area in 2017. These facts together form the basis of the inference.
steps.note: The word 'notwithstanding' is the pivot — it explicitly connects the illegality to the purchase action.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A
steps.apply: Option A claims farmers act only if India benefits. The passage says nothing about national benefit being a farmer motivation. This adds an assumption not in the text.

steps.title: Evaluate Option B
steps.apply: Option B states farmers want the technology even if it is not legal. The passage directly supports this: technology is not legal + farmers bought and planted it anyway. This is a clean logical inference.

steps.title: Evaluate Options C and D
steps.apply: Option C (experimental purposes): 15% of cotton area is large-scale adoption, not an experiment. Option D (paying high price): While true, paying a premium is a supporting detail; the key inference is about legality, not price. D is incomplete as the primary inference.

solution.result: B. Farmers want to access the new technology even if it is not legal.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal reasoning question based on passage comprehension; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
"Popular Hindi fiction, despite - or perhaps because of - its wide reach, often does not appear in our cinema. As ideals that viewers are meant to look up to rather identify with, Hindi film protagonists usually read books of aspirational value: textbook, English books, or high-value literature."

Which one of the following CANNOT be inferred from the above passage?

A. Though popular Hindi fiction has wide reach, it often does not appear in the movies.
B. Protagonists in Hindi movies, being ideals for viewers, read only books of aspirational value.
C. Textbooks, English books, or high-value literature have aspirational value.
D. People do not look up to writers of books or high-value literature.
questions.to_find: statements that CANNOT be inferred from the passage

understand.plain: The passage is a quote about popular Hindi fiction and its absence from Indian cinema. It notes that Hindi film heroes are presented as ideals and therefore shown reading 'aspirational' books (textbooks, English books, high-value literature). We must find what CANNOT be inferred — meaning statements that add information not present or contradict what is stated.

keywords.explain: The statement either contradicts the passage, adds assumptions not in the text, or overstates the original claim.
keywords.example: If the passage says protagonists 'usually' read aspirational books, saying they read 'only' such books is an overstatement.

keywords.explain: Books that viewers aspire toward — textbooks, English books, high-value literature — symbolise achievement and status.
keywords.example: Showing a hero reading an English textbook signals upward mobility and intellectual aspiration.

keywords.explain: The passage distinguishes heroes as ideals (to look up to) rather than realistic figures to identify with, explaining the aspirational book choices.
keywords.example: An everyday reader might identify with popular Hindi fiction; a film hero is shown reading English to inspire aspiration.

understand.visual_alt: Comparison of what Hindi film protagonists are shown reading versus what the general public actually reads in popular fiction.

given.aim: find which statements CANNOT be validly inferred from the quoted passage

terms.meaning: Popular Hindi fiction has wide reach but often doesn't appear in movies.
terms.example: The passage explicitly says this — directly stated fact.
terms.connects: CAN be inferred — this is a direct restatement of the passage

terms.meaning: Protagonists read ONLY books of aspirational value.
terms.example: The passage says they 'usually' read such books — 'usually' is not the same as 'only'.
terms.connects: CANNOT be inferred — 'usually' is weakened to 'only' which is an overstatement

terms.meaning: Textbooks, English books, and high-value literature have aspirational value.
terms.example: The passage explicitly calls these 'books of aspirational value: textbooks, English books, or high-value literature.'
terms.connects: CAN be inferred — directly stated in the passage

terms.meaning: People do not look up to writers of books or high-value literature.
terms.example: The passage says people look up to film PROTAGONISTS who read such books; it says nothing about attitudes toward the writers themselves.
terms.connects: CANNOT be inferred — the passage is about readers/protagonists, not about attitudes toward authors

given.plan: Since the question asks what CANNOT be inferred, identify statements A and C as directly supported, then B (overstates 'usually' as 'only') and D (introduces writers — not mentioned in the passage) as the two that cannot be inferred. The answer is B and D.

steps.title: Check statement A
steps.apply: The passage says: 'Popular Hindi fiction ... often does not appear in our cinema.' Statement A rephrases this accurately. It CAN be inferred.
steps.note: A is supported — not part of the answer.

steps.title: Check statement B
steps.apply: The passage uses the word 'usually' — 'Hindi film protagonists USUALLY read books of aspirational value.' Statement B replaces 'usually' with 'only', making it an absolute claim. 'Usually' allows for exceptions; 'only' does not. This is a logical overstatement.
steps.note: B CANNOT be inferred — 'usually' is not 'only'.

steps.title: Check statement C
steps.apply: The passage explicitly lists 'textbook, English books, or high-value literature' as examples of 'books of aspirational value.' Statement C simply restates this classification. It CAN be inferred.
steps.note: C is supported — not part of the answer.

steps.title: Check statement D
steps.apply: The passage says viewers look up to Hindi film protagonists. Statement D claims people do NOT look up to writers. The passage never discusses writers — it only discusses what the protagonists (fictional characters) read. Introducing attitudes toward writers is entirely outside the passage's scope.
steps.note: D CANNOT be inferred — writers are not mentioned in the passage.

solution.result: B and D cannot be inferred. B overstates 'usually' as 'only'; D introduces writers who are not discussed in the passage.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal reasoning / passage comprehension question; no mathematical formulas are required.
